sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 4-year-old mother of twins, injured her right knee while skiing in Vermont last weekend.
She caught her ski while coming off the lift and fell, causing her knee to pop and resulting in persistent pain.
She was unable to bear weight and had to be taken down by the ski patrol.
She reports pain on the outside of the knee, which feels like it won't hold her weight, and has decreased flexion and extension.
She was initially able to bend her knee immediately after the injury but not now.
She also experiences pain with straightening the knee.
She denies numbness or tingling in her toes and is able to feel her toes.
She is generally healthy, with no high blood pressure or diabetes.
She is taking ibuprofen for pain, which is somewhat effective.
Past medical history: The patient has no significant past medical history mentioned.
Review of systems: The patient denies any additional symptoms in other organs.
Current medications: I prescribed ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain.
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient has edema and an effusion over the right knee, with pain to palpation of the right lateral knee.
There is decreased flexion and extension of the right knee, and a positive Lachman sign.
The dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable, and there is no leg edema in the ankle.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient likely has a ruptured anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) in her right knee, which is a major ligament that helps connect and stabilize the knee joint.
Problem list: ACL injury; right knee pain; knee effusion; decreased flexion and extension of the right knee.
Plan:
I ordered a knee MRI to confirm the diagnosis and rule out any other structures that may have been damaged in the accident.
I also recommended that the patient continue taking ibuprofen 600-800 mg every 8 hours as needed for pain, and wear a knee brace to provide additional support.
I hope to have the MRI results in the next couple of days and will discuss further treatment options with the patient at that time.
Follow-up: I will see the patient again soon to discuss the MRI results and plan further treatment.
